step_by_step_thinking:
First, evaluate option A: Convex lens is used — Correct. Indirect ophthalmoscopy utilizes auxiliary lenses, including convex lenses (+90D, +60D, super series), to allow visualization of the fundus. Article references confirm use of convex lenses for examining the retina.
Option B: Image is visual and erect — False. Directly contradicts established principles of optics. In indirect ophthalmoscopy, images formed through convex lenses are inverted (not erect) due to how light bends through a positive lens when the object is beyond the focal point. Real images created by convex lenses are inverted. The description matches standard principles of geometric optics referenced in article-145061_16, where “real images” (which would occur with object outside focal length) appear upside-down. Furthermore, indirect ophthalmoscopy produces an inverted image — unlike direct methods that produce upright images.
Option C: Magnification is 4–5 times — Reasonably supported. While exact numbers aren’t explicitly stated, evidence shows high-magnification capabilities (~1.3–1.6× effective magnifications per lens type, e.g., +60D and +90D lenses), and specific mentions of “high magnification,” consistent with typical reported ranges for indirect exams (often cited at ~4–5× depending on equipment and lens).
Option D: It is so bright that regular haziness is penetrated — True. Evidence supports indirect ophthalmoscopy’s superior visibility in hazy media compared to direct observation. Reference articles show that indirect techniques penetrate haze better due to brighter illumination conditions and improved lighting dynamics.
Therefore, Option B incorrectly states that the image is "visual and erect," which violates fundamental optical laws governing convex lenses.

\boxed{B}
